frame_label: Dress Code: Traditional = Respectable, Other = Questionable
generated_comment: Dekho Ganpat, main toh kehta hoon, humari sanskriti aur maryada toh saari mein hi dikhti hai. Yeh aajkal ke jo naye fashion ke kapde hain na... achha nahi lagta. Phir agar koi ladka unko dekhe toh uski kya galti?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Ramesh, a rural farmer, links traditional attire (saree) to 'sanskriti aur maryada' (culture and decency/dignity), implying other clothes lack these qualities. He then subtly blames women wearing 'naye fashion ke kapde' for potential harassment by framing male attention as a natural consequence ('uski kya galti?'). This encodes the surface manifestation of linking clothing to character and attributing harassment to what a woman was wearing.
original_frame: Prenup, Alimony as Reverse Dowry, Women
persona_text: You are Ramesh. Age 55. Married. A farmer in rural Maharashtra. Your neighbour's son just got divorced after two years of marriage and the wife took the gold jewellery and went back to her parents. You are talking to three other farmers sitting under a tree in the afternoon. You are commenting on what happened and connecting it to a broader pattern you have observed about what women from certain kinds of families are like and why young men today should be very careful before marrying
